hallucinations.description: The paper repeatedly states that MMLU was evaluated in 4-shot format, but the provided code and logs show MMLU was actually run with 0-shot. This affects the claimed evaluation protocol and also undermines later discussion of a '4-shot vs 0-shot' asymmetry between MMLU and TruthfulQA.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper: "MMLU (cais/mmlu, full test set, 14,042 items, 57 subjects, 4-shot)" and "MMLU uses 4-shot evaluation while TruthfulQA MC1 uses 0-shot". Code/logs: in calibration_analysis.py, EVAL_CONFIG has "num_fewshot": 0; experiment_full.log shows repeated warnings "Overwriting default num_fewshot ... to 0" and the lm-eval summary lists tasks with "n-shot 0".

hallucinations.description: The paper names different fallback checkpoints than those actually used in code/results, especially for PPO and some DPO/SFT models. This is not a minor formatting issue because the paper's central claims are tied to specific checkpoints. In particular, the paper claims PPO used usvsnsp/pythia-{size}-ppo for 1.4B, but code/results used lomahony/pythia-1.4b-helpful-sfted1-ppo-3epochs-old. The paper also claims Leogrin DPO and lomahony SFT variants uniformly, while code uses different IDs for several sizes.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper §3.2/§4.1: "Publicly available fallback checkpoints were used instead: lomahony/pythia-{size}-helpful-sft (SFT), Leogrin/eleuther-pythia{size}-hh-dpo (DPO), and usvsnsp/pythia-{size}-ppo (PPO)." Code MODEL_REGISTRY / ALIGNED_MODEL_IDS: "1.4b-ppo": "lomahony/pythia-1.4b-helpful-sfted1-ppo-3epochs-old"; "2.8b-dpo": "lomahony/eleuther-pythia2.8b-hh-dpo"; "6.9b-sft": "lomahony/eleuther-pythia6.9b-hh-sft"; results files are saved under those actual IDs.

hallucinations.description: The headline H2 result is invalidated by a model-loading failure for the key 1.4B-PPO checkpoint. The logs show that this PPO checkpoint was not loaded as a compatible causal LM: many checkpoint weights were unused and many GPTNeoX weights were newly initialized, with an explicit warning that the model should probably be trained before use. Yet the paper treats the resulting 1.4B-PPO metrics as substantive evidence, including the strongest claims in the paper (negative rho, 99.7% argmax change). Those results are from a misloaded or partially random model, not a legitimate aligned checkpoint evaluation.
hallucinations.evidence: experiment_full.log for pythia-1.4b-ppo: "Some weights of the model checkpoint at lomahony/pythia-1.4b-helpful-sfted1-ppo-3epochs-old were not used when initializing GPTNeoXForCausalLM..." followed by "Some weights of GPTNeoXForCausalLM were not initialized ... and are newly initialized ... You should probably TRAIN this model on a down-stream task to be able to use it for predictions and inference." Paper §5.3: "The 1.4B-PPO case is particularly notable: rho = -0.324 ... 99.7% of MMLU items have a different top-ranked option after alignment."

hallucinations.description: The paper incorrectly describes the Brier score decomposition, claiming that higher resolution indicates better calibration. In Murphy's decomposition, reliability is the calibration term, while resolution measures sharpness/discrimination, not calibration itself. Calling higher resolution 'better calibration' is mathematically wrong.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper §3.4: "Brier Score Decomposition (Murphy, 1973): - Reliability: the overconfidence term. Higher values indicate greater overconfidence. - Resolution: discriminability. Higher values indicate better calibration."

hallucinations.description: At least one cited work appears to be misattributed or nonexistent in the stated form. The reference for 'Chhikara, P. (2025). Mind the Confidence Gap: Overconfidence, Calibration, and Distractor Effects in Large Language Models. arXiv:2502.11028.' is suspicious because the paper text cites 'Chhikara et al. (2025)' but the references list only a single author and no corroborating evidence is provided in code or paper. This looks like a fabricated or malformed citation entry.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper §2.3: "Chhikara et al. (2025) studied calibration using verbally elicited confidence..." References: "Chhikara, P. (2025). Mind the Confidence Gap: Overconfidence, Calibration, and Distractor Effects in Large Language Models. arXiv:2502.11028."

overall_assessment: Hallucinations were found. The most serious issue is that the paper's strongest mechanism claim relies on a misloaded 1.4B-PPO model, making those reported results unreliable. There are also clear methodology mismatches (4-shot vs actual 0-shot MMLU, incorrect checkpoint identities), plus a mathematical misstatement and at least one dubious citation. These issues materially weaken the paper.
